does: This paper introduces ProcessBench, a benchmark dataset of 3,400 test cases designed to evaluate models' ability to identify erroneous steps in mathematical reasoning. Each test case contains a step-by-step solution to competition and Olympiad-level math problems with human-annotated error locations. The task requires models to identify the earliest step containing an error or determine that all steps are correct. The authors evaluate two types of error-identification approaches: process reward models (PRMs) that directly predict error locations, and critic models that use prompted language models to critique each solution step. The evaluation reveals that existing PRMs struggle with generalization beyond simpler datasets like GSM8K and MATH, while prompted language models show stronger performance on harder problems despite lacking specialized training.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on scalable oversight and automated verification of AI reasoning, extending prior work on process reward models used for reasoning supervision. ProcessBench responds to limitations of existing error-detection benchmarks that focus primarily on grade-school and competition mathematics at moderate difficulty levels. The paper connects to recent research on step-level evaluation of language model reasoning, building on frameworks like those used in math problem-solving datasets such as MATH and GSM8K. It also relates to broader efforts in mechanistic interpretability and AI safety that aim to understand and correct reasoning errors in large language models. The comparison between specialized PRMs and general critic models extends discussions about whether task-specific fine-tuning or few-shot prompting better enables reliable reasoning verification.
enables: ProcessBench establishes a foundation for developing more robust error-detection methods for mathematical reasoning, which could improve safety and reliability of language models in high-stakes domains. The benchmark enables future research on training PRMs that generalize to harder problems, potentially through novel architectures or training procedures that capture reasoning patterns across difficulty levels. It supports the development of critic models as an alternative to PRMs for scalable oversight, examining whether general capabilities can be leveraged for step-level verification. The work points toward building better human-AI collaborative systems where models can identify their own reasoning errors, enabling efficient error correction and learning. Finally, it provides empirical grounding for studying the trade-offs between specialized supervision (trained PRMs) and general capabilities (prompted critique) in reasoning verification, with implications for designing scalable oversight mechanisms for advanced AI systems.
